perturbation.original_text: This Agreem ent shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of California. The federal and state courts within the State of Calif ornia shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to return to the Company immediately upon the Company’s written request all Confidential Information, including but not limited to all computer programs, documentation, notes, plans, drawings, and copies thereof. Recipient agrees to comply with all United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information. Recipient hereby acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information could cause irreparable harm an d significant injury which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that the Company shall have the right to seek and obtain immediate injunctive relief from breaches of this Agreement, in addition to any other rights and remedies it may have. This Agreem ent shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of Delaware. The federal and state courts within the State of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to adjudicate any dispute arising out of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law and jurisdiction clause to a location far from other legal sections of the document can reduce its prominence. Furthermore, changing the applicable state from California to Delaware, without properly reflecting the parties' connections to Delaware, introduces a conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10 allows California courts to exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of California or the United States. By stating Delaware law applies exclusively, when the agreement originates and is largely performed in California, there is a potential conflict about whether California courts can exert jurisdiction, even if constitutional requirements are met. Delaware law may be less favorable to the plaintiff (VMware) than California law, making the agreement more difficult to enforce in VMware's home state.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 1. Jurisdiction [410.10- 410.10.] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) 410.10. A court of this state may exercise jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the Constitution of this state or of the United States. (Added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by altering the governing law and jurisdiction from California to Delaware. While California Code of Civil Procedure § 410.10, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants California courts jurisdiction on any basis not inconsistent with the state or federal constitutions, the changed text seeks to establish Delaware law as exclusively governing, potentially undermining California's jurisdictional reach and introducing a structural flaw as defined in the Perturbation Definitions. This directly affects the enforceability of the contract by creating uncertainty and a potential legal conflict regarding which state's laws and courts should govern disputes.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to comply with all United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: BY CLICKING THE “YES, I AGREE” BUTTON ON THE EXAM ENTRY SCREEN, YOU STATE THAT YOU HAVE READ AND UNDERSTAND THIS AGREEMENT AND THAT YOU AGREE TO BE BOUND BY ITS TERMS AND C ONDITIONS. Recipient agrees to comply with all United States export controls with respect to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Placing the export compliance clause near the 'I Agree' button obfuscates its importance and creates doubt if the user agreeing to take the exam is also agreeing to the export control terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.1 requires that exporters know their responsibilities under Export Administration Regulations (EAR). By burying the export control compliance clause near the agreement confirmation, there is a higher chance that recipients may not fully understand that they are explicitly agreeing to comply with US export control laws related to the confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The added text, particularly the placement of the compliance clause near the 'I Agree' button, introduces a structural flaw by obfuscating the importance of export compliance, making it less likely the recipient will fully understand they are agreeing to comply with US export control laws related to confidential information. This contradicts the spirit, if not the precise letter, of 15 CFR § 732.1, which requires exporters to know their responsibilities under the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), by burying it in the agreement. Snippet 1 mentions that the eCFR contains authoritative content, which supports using the 15 CFR § 732.1 as a legally grounded source. The change significantly weakens understanding of export control obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information could cause irreparable harm an d significant injury which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that the Company shall have the right to seek and obtain immediate injunctive relief from breaches of this Agreement, in addition to any other rights and remedies it may have.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information remains the property of the Company and no license or other rights in the Confidential Informati on is granted hereby. All information is provided “as is” and without any warranty, express, implied, or otherwise, regarding its accuracy or performance. Recipient hereby acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information could cause only minor harm and d insignificant injury which may be difficult to ascertain. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that the Company shall have the right to seek and obtain monetary relief equal to the value of the Confidential Information if there are breaches of this Agreement, in addition to any other rights and remedies it may have.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'irreparable harm and significant injury' to 'minor harm and insignificant injury' directly contradicts the need for injunctive relief, weakening VMware's ability to quickly prevent further disclosure. Also, changing the remedy to monetary relief limits VMware's options.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3422 states that an injunction cannot be granted when monetary relief would be adequate to protect the complaining party. By changing the text to state unauthorized disclosure only causes minor harm, the agreement suggests that monetary damages would be sufficient, directly weakening the claim for injunctive relief that VMware would need to prevent irreparable harm.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3422 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. Preventive Relief [3420 - 3424] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 3422. Except where otherwise provided by this Title, a final injunction may be granted to prevent the breach of an obligation existing in favor of the applicant: 1. Where pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief; 2. Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief; 3. Where the restraint is necessary to prevent a multiplicity of judicial proceedings; or, 4. Where the obligation arises from a trust. (Enacted 1872.)

(Enacted 1872.) Last modified: October 25, 2018
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 3422. The original text acknowledges 'irreparable harm and significant injury', justifying injunctive relief. The changed text reduces this to 'minor harm and insignificant injury' and proposes 'monetary relief', directly contradicting the premise for injunctive relief. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2 both state that an injunction may be granted where 'pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief'. By suggesting only minor harm and proposing monetary relief, the changed text implies that monetary compensation would be adequate, thus undermining the basis for an injunction. This creates a clear contradiction, making the modified clause likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreem ent shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that all information disclosed by the Company to Recipient, including witho ut limitation information acquired by Recipient from Company employees or inspection of the Company’s property, relating to (without limitation) the Company’s products, designs, business plans, business opportunities, finances, research, development, know -how or personnel, and confidential information disclosed to the Company by third parties, shall be considered Confidential Information. This Agreem ent shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada as they apply to contracts entered into and wholly to be performed in the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada, and then stating that it applies to contracts wholly performed in Nevada, introduces a legal contradiction since VMware is based in California, and the contract is most likely to be executed and primarily performed in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 states that the law of the state chosen by the parties to govern their contractual rights and duties will be applied if the particular issue is one which the parties could have resolved by an explicit provision in their agreement directed to that issue and the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or there is any other reasonable basis for the parties' choice. If Nevada has no substantial relationship to the parties (VMware and the recipient) or the transaction, then this choice-of-law provision may be invalid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The change of governing law from California to Nevada, especially when the contract's performance is likely in California, creates a contradiction. The LLM Law Explanation references Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, indicating that the chosen state's law applies only if it has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that VMware is based in California, making Nevada's relevance questionable. The changed text introduces a structural flaw that could render the choice-of-law provision invalid, due to its contradiction with established conflict of laws principles. Snippet 1 provides a definition of Conflict of laws, which further supports the analysis that changing the jurisdiction has legal consequences that should be evaluated.
